definition: Matrix multiplication combines an m x n matrix A and an n x p matrix B into an m x p matrix C where each entry Ci,j is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.
layman: Matrix multiplication takes two step-by-step linear operations and fuses them into a single operation using a rows-times-columns rule.
content:
## What matrix multiplication does

Matrix multiplication is the operation that multiplies two compatible matrices A and B to produce a new matrix C that represents doing B first and then A as one combined linear transformation.
If A has shape m x n and B has shape n x p, then the product C = AB has shape m x p.
Each entry Ci,j is computed by taking row i of A, column j of B, multiplying corresponding entries and summing them.

Formally, if A is m x n and B is n x p, then for each i in 1..m and j in 1..p,

- Ci,j = sum over k = 1..n of Ai,k * Bk,j (a dot product between row i of A and column j of B).

This row-by-column rule is what makes matrix multiplication match the composition of linear maps.

## Dimension rule: when AB is defined

Matrix multiplication AB is defined exactly when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B.
This shared size is the inner dimension.

- If A is m x n and B is n x p, then AB is defined and has shape m x p.
- If A is m x n and B is p x n with p != n, then AB is not defined because the inner dimensions do not match.

A useful habit is to annotate matrices with shapes while you work, for example X (batch x features), W (features x outputs), so you can see at a glance that XW has shape batch x outputs.

## Algebraic properties

Matrix multiplication enjoys several key algebraic properties that mirror familiar scalar arithmetic, but with important differences.

- Associative: (AB)C = A(BC) whenever all products are defined.
- Distributive over addition: A(B + C) = AB + AC and (A + B)C = AC + BC when shapes are compatible.
- Not commutative in general: AB is usually not equal to BA, and often one of AB or BA is not even defined.

There is also a multiplicative identity.
For each size n, the identity matrix In is the n x n matrix with ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere.
Whenever dimensions match, AIn = A and ImA = A for an m x n matrix A.

## Geometric and transformational view

Every matrix can be viewed as a linear transformation between vector spaces.
If B maps vectors from Rp to Rn and A maps vectors from Rn to Rm, then AB is the linear map from Rp to Rm that first applies B and then applies A.

In 2D or 3D geometry this becomes concrete:

- A rotation matrix R rotates vectors.
- A scaling matrix S stretches or shrinks vectors along axes.
- The product SR represents "rotate, then scale" as one matrix; RS generally represents a different transformation because order matters.

In machine learning, weight matrices in linear or neural network layers play the same role: multiplying by W applies a linear transformation to feature vectors.
Chaining layers multiplies several matrices, composing many linear transformations into one.

## Row and column perspectives

Two complementary perspectives on AB help build intuition.

- Row view: each row of AB is a linear combination of the rows of B, where the coefficients come from the corresponding row of A.
- Column view: each column of AB is a linear combination of the columns of A, where the coefficients come from the corresponding column of B.

The row view is handy when you think of B as storing feature templates and A as mixing them to produce new rows.
The column view is natural when you think of A as sending basis vectors (columns) to new directions and B as providing coordinates in that basis.

Taken together, these views explain why changing one element of A or B can affect an entire row or column of the product C.
keyPoints: AB is defined when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B; the result keeps the outer dimensions (rows of A, columns of B).
Each entry Ci,j in AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B, not a simple element-wise product.
Matrix multiplication composes linear transformations, is associative and distributive over addition, but is generally not commutative.
The identity matrix acts as a multiplicative identity AI = A and IA = A whenever dimensions match.
